title: Turmeric golden milk
subtitle: A warming blend for after meals
excerpt: Ground turmeric simmered in whole milk with black pepper and a little fat to improve absorption. A traditional Ayurvedic preparation that the western kitchen has adopted as a warming digestive drink.
sourceNotes: Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India, Curcuma longa reference entry, the milk-based preparations (haldi doodh) and fat-pepper combination. the World Health Organisation Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 1, Rhizoma Curcumae Longae, the digestive and inflammation-calming tradition. the herbal reference on Curcumae longae rhizoma, well-established use for mild digestive complaints.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ground turmeric stores 2 years in an airtight tin away from light and heat. Prepare each cup fresh; the milk combination does not keep well.

glossaryTerms.definition: The active compound in black pepper that substantially increases curcumin absorption (bioavailability). The traditional combination of turmeric with black pepper and fat is now understood to work through piperine's inhibition of gut metabolism.

content.text: Haldi doodh, turmeric milk, golden milk: this preparation is the Ayurvedic kitchen's answer to a warming digestive drink. Ground turmeric (1 teaspoon, about 3-4 g) simmered in whole milk with a pinch of black pepper and a small amount of fat (ghee or butter). The combination is deliberate and functional: the

in black pepper and the fat both improve the absorption of curcumin, the curcuminoid with the most studied activity. Without them, most of the curcumin passes through unabsorbed. The preparation takes five minutes, keeps the kitchen warm in winter, and sits well after a heavy meal.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Anticoagulants. Curcumin has mild antiplatelet activity. Readers on warfarin, aspirin at therapeutic dose, clopidogrel, or similar should keep turmeric to culinary amounts (a pinch in cooking) and consult their prescriber before a daily cup of golden milk.

Gallstones. Turmeric stimulates bile flow. If you have diagnosed gallstones with bile duct obstruction, avoid concentrated preparations including daily golden milk.

Pregnancy. One cup of golden milk with a culinary amount of turmeric (1 tsp) is within the normal food-safe range throughout pregnancy. Therapeutic-dose supplements or powders (more than 1 tsp daily consistently) are outside this envelope; consult your midwife.

Paediatric use. Not a therapeutic preparation for children under twelve.

items.prepNote: or a plant milk, though fat content improves absorption
about 1 level teaspoon of ground turmeric
a generous pinch of freshly ground black pepper
about half a teaspoon of ghee; unsalted butter or coconut oil also work
optional, to sweeten; add after cooking

content.text: Combine the milk, turmeric, black pepper, and butter in a small saucepan over medium-low heat. Whisk to disperse the turmeric before the milk comes to temperature. Heat gently, whisking regularly, until the milk is steaming and just below a simmer (about 70-75°C). Do not let it boil. A rolling boil can break the milk and reduces the volatile compounds.

Simmer at this temperature for 3-4 minutes, still whisking occasionally. The milk will be deep yellow-gold and smell warmly spicy. Pour through a fine sieve into a cup (this catches any undissolved turmeric particles, though a well-whisked preparation will have very little). Add honey to taste after pouring, not during cooking.

One cup once a day, after a meal. This falls within the standard herbal dose for turmeric. Take for up to 4-6 weeks. Regular daily use is within the food-safe range for most people. It is not the same as a concentrated supplement. Not for children under twelve as a therapeutic preparation.

Prepare fresh each time. The finished drink does not keep; the milk will spoil within a day and reheating it reduces the quality of the preparation. Ground turmeric keeps 2 years in an airtight tin, cool and dark, away from steam and light.
When not to use this remedy

If you take anticoagulants, keep turmeric to culinary amounts only. If you have bile duct obstruction, avoid this preparation. See a doctor for indigestion lasting more than 2 weeks, or for indigestion with weight loss, difficulty swallowing, vomiting, or blood in the stool.
